title: Do California Agricultural Vehicles Need Clean Truck Check Testing? Yes—Here’s What You Need to Know

🌾 What Is the Clean Truck Check Program?

California’s Clean Truck Check is a statewide regulation requiring emissions testing for most diesel and alternative-fuel heavy-duty vehicles with a GVWR over 14,000 lbs. That includes many agricultural trucks, especially:

  • Grain haulers

  • Livestock carriers

  • Flatbeds and stake trucks used for crop transport

  • Truck-tractor and trailer combinations hauling to the first point of processing

Even if your vehicle is only used seasonally or stays on rural roads, you still may be required to test annually.

🧑‍🌾 What Is an Agricultural Vehicle—And Does It Need Testing?

An agricultural vehicle is defined by the California Air Resources Board (CARB) as any vehicle:

  • Owned or operated by a farming business, and

🛑 Personal-use vehicles, general transport vehicles, and those not directly supporting farming do not qualify for agricultural exemption status and still need testing if they meet Clean Truck Check criteria.

title: Clean Truck Check: What California Fleet Owners Need to Know for 2024–2025
content:
Brought to you by NorCal CARB Mobile
CARB-Credentialed Onsite Testing & Compliance Services
Serving All of California | 📞 916-890-4427 | 📧 office@norcalcarbmobile.com


What Is Clean Truck Check?

Clean Truck Check (CTC) is California's required emissions inspection and maintenance program for heavy-duty vehicles over 14,000 lbs GVWR. It ensures trucks, buses, and fleets comply with state air quality standards.

If your vehicle operates in California—even if registered in another state—CTC likely applies.

Covered Vehicles Include:

  • Diesel and alternative-fuel trucks

  • School, shuttle, and transit buses

  • Commercial hybrids

  • Government fleet vehicles

  • California-registered motorhomes

Do Any Vehicles Qualify for Exemption?

Yes. The following are not subject to Clean Truck Check:

  • Battery-electric and hydrogen vehicles

  • Tactical military vehicles

  • Emergency and historical vehicles

  • Motorhomes registered out-of-state

  • Engines certified to the cleanest NOx standard (exempt until 2027)

If you're unsure about your fleet status, call NorCal CARB Mobile for a quick eligibility review.

What’s Happening Now?

CARB began enforcement in January 2023 using roadside emissions monitoring. If your vehicle is flagged, you’ll receive a Notice to Submit to Testing (NST).

From October 1, 2024, emissions testing will be required for compliance deadlines on or after January 1, 2025. Vehicles must pass and submit a certified test within the required timeframe.

Who Can Perform Testing?

Only CARB-certified testers can perform Clean Truck Check emissions tests. That’s where NorCal CARB Mobile comes in.

title: Mobile CARB/DMV Diesel Clean Truck Checks: Compliance for Semi-Trucks in Sacramento to  Fresno Corridor
content:
Navigating California's stringent emissions regulations can be challenging for semi-truck owners and fleet operators, especially with the California Air Resources Board (CARB) Clean Truck Check program. For those operating in the Sacramento to Fresno corridor, ensuring compliance is critical to avoid fines, registration holds, and operational disruptions. Our mobile CARB/DMV Diesel Clean Truck Check service offers a seamless, cost-effective solution tailored to the needs of semi-truck operators in this region, with no travel fees added to our rates. This blog provides a comprehensive guide to the Clean Truck Check requirements, our mobile testing services, and how we help keep your fleet compliant and road-ready.

Understanding CARB’s Clean Truck Check Program

The Clean Truck Check, formally known as the Heavy-Duty Inspection and Maintenance (HD I/M) Regulation, is a CARB initiative designed to reduce emissions from heavy-duty vehicles operating in California. Enacted under Senate Bill 210 (2019), the program targets diesel and alternative fuel vehicles with a gross vehicle weight rating (GVWR) over 14,000 pounds, including semi-trucks, regardless of whether they are registered in California. The goal is to ensure that emissions control systems are functioning properly, reducing smog-forming nitrogen oxides (NOx) and particulate matter (PM) that contribute to poor air quality.

Key Compliance Requirements

To comply with the Clean Truck Check, semi-truck owners must:

  1. Register in the CTC-VIS Database: All affected vehicles must be reported in the Clean Truck Check Vehicle Inspection System (CTC-VIS) at cleantruckcheck.arb.ca.gov. This includes vehicle and owner information, such as the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN), make, model, and fuel type.

  2. Pay the Annual Compliance Fee: As of 2025, the annual fee is $31.18 per vehicle, adjusted annually based on the California Consumer Price Index (CCPI). Payments must be made through CTC-VIS using credit/debit cards or eCheck, with processing times of 1-2 days for cards and up to 7 days for eChecks.

  3. Complete Emissions Compliance Testing:

    • Vehicles with 2013 or Newer Engines: Require a visual inspection and an On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) test twice per year (increasing to quarterly testing starting January 2027 for OBD-equipped vehicles).

    • Vehicles with 2012 or Older Diesel Engines: Require a smoke opacity test and a visual inspection of emissions control equipment twice per year.

    • Alternative Fuel Vehicles (2017 or Older): Require only a visual inspection semi-annually.

    • Testing must be conducted by a CARB-credentialed tester using certified equipment, with results submitted to CARB within 90 days prior to the vehicle’s compliance deadline.

  4. Maintain Compliance Certificates: After passing tests and paying fees, a compliance certificate is issued for each vehicle, accessible via CTC-VIS. While not required to be carried, keeping certificates handy is recommended for inspections or freight facility access.

Compliance Deadlines

  • California-Registered Vehicles: Deadlines align with DMV registration expiration dates, with semi-annual testing due on the expiration date and six months later (e.g., a January 1 registration expiration requires testing by January 1 and July 1).

  • Out-of-State and DMV-Exempt Vehicles: Deadlines are based on the last digit of the VIN, with specific months assigned (e.g., VIN ending in 2 has deadlines in June and December). Check your CTC-VIS account for exact dates.

  • 2024 Testing: For 2024, only one test is required per vehicle, with deadlines between July 1 and December 31, 2024. Starting January 1, 2025, semi-annual testing is mandatory for most vehicles.

Non-Compliance Consequences

Failure to comply can result in:

  • DMV Registration Holds: Non-compliant vehicles face registration blocks, preventing renewal until compliance is met.

  • Fines: Penalties can reach up to $10,000 per vehicle per day for non-compliance.

  • Operational Restrictions: Non-compliant trucks may be restricted from operating in California, particularly at seaports and railyards.

Our Mobile Clean Truck Check Services in Sacramento to Fresno

title: Is CARB Clean Truck Check (CTC) for Diesel Trucks over 14k lbs. going away?
content:
CARB Clean Truck Check: What You Need to Know for 2013-2018 Diesel Vehicles

At NorcalCARBMobile.com, we’re committed to keeping California truck owners informed about the latest regulations. There’s been some buzz about whether the California Air Resources Board (CARB) Clean Truck Check program, also known as the Heavy-Duty Inspection and Maintenance (HD I/M) program, is going away for 2013-2018 diesel vehicles. Let’s clear the air: the Clean Truck Check is here to stay, and compliance is more critical than ever for your diesel trucks. Here’s the latest update as of July 17, 2025.

The Clean Truck Check Is Fully Enforced

Far from being phased out, the Clean Truck Check program is in full swing. As of October 1, 2024, new emissions testing requirements are in effect for heavy-duty diesel vehicles with a Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR) over 14,000 pounds operating on California roads, regardless of where they’re registered. For 2013 and newer model year diesel engines, this means biannual emissions compliance testing using a CARB-certified On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) testing device.

Starting January 1, 2025, non-compliance could lead to serious consequences, including:

  • DMV registration holds, preventing vehicle registration.

  • Fines of up to $10,000 per vehicle per day.

What About 2013-2018 Diesel Vehicles?

If you own a diesel truck from the 2013-2018 model years, the Clean Truck Check requirements apply to you. These vehicles must undergo OBD-based emissions testing twice a year. This program replaces the older Periodic Smoke Inspection Program (PSIP), so you no longer need smoke opacity tests for these model years—just the OBD test.

Looking ahead, the requirements will get stricter. Starting in October 2027, testing frequency for 2013 and newer vehicles will increase to four times per year. This signals CARB’s commitment to tightening emissions standards, not relaxing them.

Are There Any Exemptions?

There’s a limited exemption for vehicles with engines certified to the ultra-low NOx standard (0.010 g/bhp-hr), which are exempt from testing until January 1, 2027. However, as of June 1, 2024, no manufacturer has certified an engine to this standard, so this exemption doesn’t currently apply to most 2013-2018 diesel vehicles. Zero-emission vehicles are also exempt, but that’s unlikely to apply to your diesel fleet.

Why the Confusion?

Rumors about the Clean Truck Check “going away” might stem from the transition from PSIP to the new HD I/M program or misunderstandings about specific exemptions. Rest assured, the program is active, with roadside emissions monitoring and annual compliance fees (currently $30-$60 per vehicle) in place to ensure adherence.

How NorcalCARBMobile.com Can Help

At NorcalCARBMobile.com, we make compliance easy. Our mobile testing services bring CARB-certified OBD testing directly to you, saving you time and ensuring your 2013-2018 diesel vehicles meet Clean Truck Check standards. Avoid costly fines and registration issues by scheduling your biannual testing with us today.

Stay Informed

CARB continues to refine the Clean Truck Check program. To stay updated:

  • Sign up for CARB’s email alerts at arb.ca.gov.

  • Join CARB’s upcoming webinar on July 23, 2025, for the latest program details.

  • Contact CARB directly at hdim@arb.ca.gov or call their hotline at 916.890.4427.

Don’t let rumors mislead you—compliance is mandatory, and NorcalCARBMobile.com is here to keep your fleet on the road and in the clear. Schedule your Clean Truck Check today!

title: Your Guide to CARB’s Diesel Clean Truck Check: Deadlines and Requirements for 2025
content:
Navigating the new regulations from the California Air Resources Board (CARB) can be a challenge. At NorcalCARBMobile, our job is to make it simple. The Clean Truck Check program (also known as the Heavy-Duty Inspection and Maintenance program) is now in full swing, and understanding your compliance deadlines is crucial to keeping your vehicles on the road without interruption. Call 916-890-4427 with any questions.

This guide breaks down exactly what you need to know about your annual (or semi-annual) compliance requirements.

The Core Requirements: What You MUST Do

To operate a heavy-duty vehicle on California's public roads, you must be in full compliance with the Clean Truck Check program. This means:

  1. Reporting: Your vehicle must be registered in CARB’s Clean Truck Check - Vehicle Inspection System, known as CTC-VIS.

  2. Fees: The annual compliance fee for each vehicle must be paid by its deadline.

  3. Violations: Any outstanding enforcement violations for the vehicle must be cleared.

  4. Testing: For compliance deadlines of January 1, 2025, or later, a passing emissions test must be submitted.

Failure to comply can result in DMV registration blocks and other enforcement actions.

Is Your Vehicle Subject to Clean Truck Check?

The rule applies to nearly all diesel and alternative fuel heavy-duty trucks, buses, and other vehicles with a Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR) over 14,000 pounds. This includes:

  • Commercial trucks and buses

  • Hybrid vehicles

  • Privately-owned vehicles

  • Government vehicles (state, local, and federal)

  • Vehicles registered outside of California that operate in the state

Special Note for Motor Homes & Agricultural Vehicles: California-registered motor homes used for recreational or emergency purposes, along with designated agricultural vehicles, are only required to test once annually. However, motor homes used for commercial purposes must follow the semi-annual compliance schedule detailed below.

Finding Your Compliance Deadline

Your vehicle's compliance deadline depends on where it is registered.

A. For California-Registered Vehicles

If your vehicle is registered with the California DMV (or was historically registered in CA), your compliance deadline aligns with your DMV registration expiration month.

To find your specific due date, log into your vehicle's CTC-VIS account.

Table I: Compliance Deadlines for California-Registered Vehicles | DMV Registration Expiration Month | Compliance Deadline (Fee & Test Due) | | :------------------------------ | :----------------------------------- | | January | January | | February | February | | March | March | | April | April | | May | May | | June | June | | July | July | | August | August | | September | September | | October | October | | November | November | | December | December |

B. For Non-California Registered & DMV-Exempt Vehicles

For the following vehicles, the compliance deadline is determined by the last digit of the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN). These vehicles must demonstrate compliance every six months.

  • Vehicles registered outside of California (that were not previously CA-registered).

  • DMV exempt-plated vehicles (e.g., state and local government fleets).

  • Federal government vehicles.

Table II: Compliance Deadlines for Non-CA Registered & DMV-Exempt Vehicles | Last Digit of VIN | Initial & Recurring Compliance Deadline | | :---------------- | :-------------------------------------- | | 0 | October | | 1 | November | | 2 | December | | 3 | January | | 4 | February | | 5 | March | | 6 | April | | 7 | May | | 8 | June | | 9 | July |

Understanding the Emissions Test

The emissions testing requirement officially begins for compliance deadlines on or after January 1, 2025. However, testing can begin as early as October 1, 2024.

A crucial planning tip: You can submit your passing emissions test up to 90 days before your vehicle's compliance deadline. For example, if your deadline is February 1, 2025, you can get tested and submit the results as early as November 3, 2024. This gives you ample time for any necessary repairs.

The type of test required depends on your vehicle's engine:

  • OBD-Equipped Vehicles: This applies to model year 2013 and newer diesel engines and 2018 and newer alternative fuel engines. The test is a simple scan of the engine’s On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) data using a CARB-certified OBD testing device.

  • Non-OBD Vehicles:

    • Diesel (2012 & older): Requires a smoke opacity test and a visual Vehicle Emissions Control Equipment Inspection.

    • Alternative Fuel (2017 & older): Requires only the visual Vehicle Emissions Control Equipment Inspection.

All testing must be performed by a CARB-credentialed tester.

Stay Compliant, Stay on the Road

The Clean Truck Check program is a new reality for vehicle operators in California. Being proactive is the best strategy. Know your deadline, schedule your test early within the 90-day window, and ensure your fees are paid.

Have questions or need to schedule your test? The certified team at NorcalCARBMobile.com is here to help. Contact us today to ensure your fleet remains compliant and operational.

title: Is Your Truck an 'Agricultural Vehicle'? Your CARB Clean Truck Check Guide
content:
For California’s vital agricultural community, keeping your vehicles compliant shouldn't be a roadblock. The California Air Resources Board (CARB) has specific rules for agricultural vehicles under the new Clean Truck Check program, and the good news is they are simpler than the requirements for other heavy-duty vehicles.

At Norcal CARB Mobile, we work with farmers and ranchers across Northern California. This guide will help you determine if your vehicle qualifies for the agricultural designation and walk you through the exact steps for compliance.

The Big Question: What Does CARB Consider an "Agricultural Vehicle"?

This is the most important step. To qualify for the simpler annual testing schedule, your vehicle must meet a specific definition. It's not just any truck used on a farm.

According to CARB, an "agricultural vehicle" is one that is:

  • Owned or operated by a farming business.

  • Used exclusively in agricultural operations. This means it cannot be a personal use vehicle.

  • Used to transport agricultural products from the field to the first point of processing.

Understanding the "first point of processing" is critical. It is the very first place where a product is altered, packaged, or prepared.

Examples of a First Point of Processing: ✔ Packinghouses ✔ Slaughterhouses ✔ Cotton gins ✔ Nut hullers/shellers ✔ Lumber mills ✔ Dehydrators and grain mills

What is NOT a First Point of Processing: ✘ Distribution centers ✘ Wholesale or retail sales locations ✘ Livestock auction houses ✘ Any location for canning or processing that occurs after the product has already been to its first processing stop.

If your vehicle's use fits this narrow definition, you can proceed with the streamlined compliance path.

Compliance Steps for Agricultural Vehicles

If your vehicle qualifies, your compliance checklist is straightforward.

  1. Report Your Vehicle: All subject vehicles (non-gasoline, GVWR >14,000 lbs) must be reported in CARB's CTC-VIS portal at cleantruckcheck.arb.ca.gov. (CARB recommends using the Microsoft Edge browser for this.)

  2. Pay the Annual Fee: Your annual compliance fee is due by the deadline shown in your CTC-VIS account. Payments must be made online via credit card, debit card, or eCheck. Paper checks are not accepted.

  3. Pass an Annual Emissions Test: This is the key benefit. Agricultural vehicles only require one test per year.

    • Testing is required for all compliance deadlines on or after January 1, 2025.

    • You can test up to 90 days before your deadline to allow time for repairs.

    • The test must be performed by a Clean Truck Check credentialed tester (like the experts at NorcalCARBMobile).

How-To Guide: Designating and Attesting Your Ag Vehicle in CTC-VIS

You must formally report and attest that your vehicle meets the agricultural definition within the CTC-VIS portal. Here’s how:

Part 1: Designating the Vehicle

When you are first adding your vehicle to the CTC-VIS system, the process is simple. In Step 5 of the vehicle reporting form, you will see a checkbox.

  • Be sure to check the box that states, "This VIN is an Agricultural Vehicle."

Part 2: Uploading the Attestation Form

After designating the vehicle, you must upload a signed form attesting to its use.

  1. Log into your CTC-VIS account. Find the vehicle under "My entity" and "Vehicles".

  2. Click Action next to the correct VIN and choose Edit Vehicle.

  3. Proceed to Step 6 of the vehicle form. You will see highlighted text to download the official attestation form. Click it and save the form to your computer.

  4. Fill out the form completely and accurately, then sign it.

  5. Back in Step 6 of the CTC-VIS form, click where it says Missing to upload your completed and signed form.

  6. Proceed to Step 7, click the checkbox to digitally sign the submission, and click Submit.

Don't Get a DMV Hold!

The California DMV will automatically place a registration hold on any vehicle that is not compliant with Clean Truck Check. To avoid this, ensure your fees are paid, your attestation is submitted, and your annual emissions test is passed on time. Your official deadlines are always the ones listed in your CTC-VIS account.

title: Complete Guide to California's CARB Clean Truck Check Program
content:
What Every Truck Owner Needs to Know in 2025

If you operate a diesel truck weighing over 14,000 lbs GVWR in California, the Clean Truck Check (CTC) isn't optional—it's the law. This comprehensive guide breaks down everything you need to know about staying compliant and avoiding costly penalties.

What is the Clean Truck Check?

The Clean Truck Check is California's mandatory inspection program for heavy-duty diesel vehicles. Implemented by the California Air Resources Board (CARB), this program ensures that emission control systems on diesel trucks are functioning properly. Think of it as an advanced smog check specifically designed for commercial vehicles.

Who Needs a Clean Truck Check?

Your vehicle requires a Clean Truck Check if it meets these criteria:

  • Diesel-powered

  • Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR) of 14,001 lbs or more

  • Operating in California (even if registered out-of-state)

  • Model year 2013 or newer (as of 2025)

This includes semi-trucks, box trucks, dump trucks, tow trucks, and many service vehicles. Even if you're just passing through California for deliveries, you're required to comply.

Testing Frequency and Deadlines

Vehicles must be tested twice per year, with inspections at least 90 days apart. The specific testing periods depend on your license plate's last digit:

  • Plates ending in 1-5: January 1 - June 30 and July 1 - December 31

  • Plates ending in 6-0: April 1 - September 30 and October 1 - March 31

Missing your testing window results in automatic non-compliance, potentially leading to DMV registration holds and DOT citations.

What Happens During the Test?

The inspection takes approximately 15-20 minutes and includes:

  • Connecting to your vehicle's OBD port

  • Running diagnostic scans of emission control systems

  • Checking for active fault codes

  • Verifying emission equipment functionality

  • Visual inspection of exhaust components

At NorCal CARB Mobile, we bring all necessary equipment directly to your location, eliminating downtime and keeping your trucks earning.

Consequences of Non-Compliance

Failing to maintain Clean Truck Check compliance can result in:

  • $1,000+ roadside citations

  • DMV registration blocks

  • DOT operating authority suspension

  • Inability to renew commercial insurance

  • Loss of contracts requiring CARB compliance

Mobile Testing Advantage

Why waste hours driving to a shop and waiting in line? Our mobile service means:

  • No lost revenue from truck downtime

  • Testing at your convenience

  • Fleet discounts starting at $50 per truck

  • Same-day compliance certificates

Don't wait until it's too late. California enforcement is increasing, and the penalties aren't worth the risk. Schedule your mobile Clean Truck Check today and keep your business moving forward.

title: How to Calculate 90 days for CARB testing compliance
content:
Navigating the Clean Truck Check: Understanding Your 90-Day Testing Window

The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is emphasizing the importance of timely emissions testing for heavy-duty vehicles, specifically reminding vehicle owners and testers about the critical 90-day testing window for the Clean Truck Check (CTC) program. To ensure your vehicle's emissions compliance test is successfully applied, it's crucial to understand how this window is calculated.

**What You Need to Know:**

* **Test Early, But Within the Window:** While CARB encourages vehicle owners to test as early as possible, this must be done within the 90-day window leading up to your specific testing deadline.

* **Accuracy is Key:** Any emissions compliance test submitted more than 90 days before your vehicle's deadline will **not** be accepted. This means the test will be invalid, and you'll be required to re-test your vehicle within the correct 90-day period.

* **CTC-VIS is Your Guide:** Your 90-day window is determined by the specific testing deadline shown in your vehicle's CTC-VIS account. Do not assume the 90-day window is simply three calendar months, as this can lead to inaccuracies.

* **Testers' Recommendation:** While testers are not responsible for determining if a vehicle is within its 90-day window, CARB recommends that testers confirm the CTC-VIS test due date with the vehicle owner before scheduling a test. This proactive step can help prevent re-testing and ensure a smooth process for everyone involved.

**Helpful Resource:**

For a visual guide on how to view your test details and due date within CTC-VIS, CARB has provided an instructional video: [How to: View Test Details/Due Date](https://youtu.be/k7iHL_AqmjQ)

By understanding and adhering to these guidelines, vehicle owners can ensure their compliance tests are valid, avoiding unnecessary re-testing and penalties. Always refer to your CTC-VIS account for the most accurate information regarding your vehicle's testing deadline.

title: Top 5 Reasons Trucks Fail Clean Truck Check (And How to Avoid Them)
content:
Blog Post 2: Top 5 Reasons Trucks Fail Clean Truck Check (And How to Avoid Them)

Prevent Costly Failures with These Pro Tips Call 916-890-4427

After conducting hundreds of Clean Truck Check inspections across Northern California, we've identified the most common failure points. Here's what causes trucks to fail and how you can avoid these expensive problems.

1. Ignored Check Engine Lights (40% of failures)

That amber warning light isn't just a suggestion. Any active emission-related fault codes will cause an automatic failure. Many operators ignore intermittent lights, hoping they'll go away.

Prevention: Address check engine lights immediately. Even if the truck seems to run fine, emission faults can worsen quickly. A simple sensor replacement today could save you from a major SCR system repair tomorrow.

2. Deferred DEF System Maintenance (25% of failures)

Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF) systems are notorious for problems when neglected. Crystallized DEF, faulty sensors, and clogged injectors are common issues we see.

Prevention:

  • Use only high-quality DEF from sealed containers

  • Replace DEF filters per manufacturer specifications

  • Don't let DEF tanks run empty

  • Address DEF warning messages immediately

3. Modified or Tampered Emission Equipment (20% of failures)

Any modifications to emission control systems result in automatic failure. This includes:

  • Deleted or bypassed DPF systems

  • EGR deletes or blocks

  • Modified ECU programming

  • Aftermarket exhaust modifications

Prevention: Keep all emission equipment stock and properly maintained. The short-term performance gains aren't worth the legal liability and compliance issues.

4. DPF Regeneration Issues (10% of failures)

Diesel Particulate Filters need regular regeneration cycles to function properly. Short trips and idle time prevent proper regeneration, leading to clogged filters.

Prevention:

  • Allow trucks to complete regeneration cycles

  • Schedule periodic forced regenerations

  • Consider manual cleaning every 100,000-150,000 miles

  • Monitor backpressure readings

5. Outdated or Incompatible Software (5% of failures)

Manufacturer software updates often address emission control issues. Running outdated ECU software can trigger fault codes that cause test failures.

Prevention: Stay current with manufacturer service bulletins and software updates. Many emission-related updates are covered under warranty.

Pro Tips for Test Day Success:

  1. Pre-test your truck: We offer preliminary scans that identify issues before official testing

  2. Clear historical codes: After repairs, ensure codes are properly cleared

  3. Warm up the engine: Cold engines may not test properly

  4. Check your paperwork: Ensure registration and insurance are current

The Mobile Advantage

When you schedule with NorCal CARB Mobile, we can perform a preliminary scan before the official test. If we identify issues, you'll know exactly what needs fixing—no surprises, no failed test on your record.

Remember: A failed Clean Truck Check doesn't just mean inconvenience; it means lost revenue, potential citations, and rushed repairs. Preventive maintenance is always cheaper than emergency fixes.

title: Motorhome CARB Testing in California: Your Complete Guide
content:
Motorhome Annual CARB Smoke Opacity Testing in California: Your Complete Guide

If you own a diesel motorhome in California, understanding CARB (California Air Resources Board) testing requirements is essential for keeping your RV road-legal. Whether you're a full-time RVer, weekend warrior, or vacation traveler, staying compliant with California's emissions standards doesn't have to be complicated.

What is CARB Smoke Opacity Testing for Motorhomes and older diesel truck engines pre- 2013?

[caption id="" align="alignnone" width="2500"] Click the image above to see engine tag photo you would need to pass the CARB Smoke Opacity Test. We can order one from manufacturer if needed [/caption]

CARB testing, also known as smoke opacity testing, measures the amount of visible smoke your diesel motorhome emits during operation. This test ensures your vehicle meets California's strict air quality standards designed to protect public health and the environment. The test measures opacity as a percentage – the higher the percentage, the more smoke your engine is producing.

Who Needs CARB Testing?

California requires periodic CARB testing for:

  • Diesel motorhomes and RVs with a gross vehicle weight rating (GVWR) over 14,000 pounds

  • Commercial vehicles including converted buses and large RVs

  • Fleet vehicles operating in California

  • Out-of-state vehicles seeking California registration

The testing frequency varies based on your vehicle's age, weight class, and registration status, but most motorhomes require testing every one to two years.

Understanding the Testing Process

During a CARB test, certified technicians use specialized equipment like the Wager 6500+ opacity meter to measure exhaust emissions during a standardized "snap-acceleration" test. Your motorhome must achieve an opacity reading below the established threshold (typically 40% for most vehicles) to pass.

Common reasons motorhomes fail CARB testing:

  • Dirty or clogged fuel injectors

  • Carbon buildup in the engine

  • Worn air filters or intake system issues

  • Turbocharger problems

  • Poor fuel quality

  • Lack of proper maintenance

Why Choose Professional Mobile CARB Testing?

Traditional testing requires driving your large motorhome to a fixed testing facility – often in busy urban areas with challenging parking and maneuvering. Mobile CARB testing brings the certified equipment and expertise directly to your location, whether you're at home, an RV park, or storage facility.

Coverage: Serving the major transportation corridors where motorhomes frequently travel – from the Sacramento Valley to the San Francisco Bay Area and Central Valley.

Preparing Your Motorhome for Testing

To maximize your chances of passing CARB testing:

  • Ensure your engine is properly warmed up before testing

  • Use quality diesel fuel from reputable stations

  • Consider fuel system treatments if you've had starting or performance issues

  • Keep up with regular maintenance including air and fuel filter changes

  • Address any check engine lights or performance concerns before testing

What Happens If You Don't Pass?

If your motorhome doesn't meet opacity standards, don't panic. Many issues can be resolved with proper maintenance or minor repairs. Common solutions include fuel system cleaning, injector service, or addressing intake system problems. We can provide guidance on next steps and retest once repairs are completed.

title: On the Road: Opacity Testing & Visual Inspections Across Northern California's Major Corridors

What is Opacity Testing?

Opacity testing measures the darkness or density of exhaust smoke coming from diesel vehicles. In simple terms, it determines how much light can pass through the exhaust emissions. Higher opacity readings indicate darker smoke, which typically means higher levels of particulate matter pollution—something CARB regulations aim to minimize.

Our mobile opacity testing equipment uses light-sensing technology to precisely measure this opacity percentage. California regulations typically require most diesel vehicles to maintain opacity levels below 5%, with some older vehicles allowed slightly higher thresholds.

What are Visual Inspections?

Visual inspections are complementary to opacity tests and involve a trained technician examining your vehicle's emission control systems to ensure all components are present, properly connected, and functioning as designed. During a visual inspection, we check for:

  • Tampering or modifications to emission control systems

  • Proper function of the Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) system

  • Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) condition

  • Proper labeling and engine certification

  • Evidence of excessive smoke or fluid leaks

title: Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) Program
content:
Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) Program Study Guide

Instructions: Answer the following questions in 2-3 sentences each.

  1. What is the primary objective of California's Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) program?

  2. Which legislative bill mandated the creation of the Clean Truck Check program, and when was it approved by CARB?

  3. What are the key components or strategies that the Clean Truck Check program employs to achieve its goals?

  4. When did the implementation of the Clean Truck Check program begin, and what is its expected outcome when fully implemented?

  5. What is the role of NORCAL CARB MOBILE in the context of the Clean Truck Check program?

  6. Describe the unique selling proposition of NORCAL CARB MOBILE's service for heavy-duty vehicle owners.

  7. How does NORCAL CARB MOBILE incentivize referrals for the Clean Truck Check service?

  8. What is the minimum cost for large fleets using NORCAL CARB MOBILE's services?

  9. Besides periodic vehicle testing, what other emissions monitoring techniques does the Clean Truck Check program incorporate?

  10. Who is the primary contact for inquiries regarding the Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) program at the California Air Resources Board?

Answer Key

  1. What is the primary objective of California's Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) program? The primary objective of the Clean Truck Check program is to ensure that heavy-duty vehicles' emissions control systems are properly functioning when traveling on California's roadways. This is crucial for achieving significant reductions in smog-forming and carcinogenic toxic air pollution.

  2. Which legislative bill mandated the creation of the Clean Truck Check program, and when was it approved by CARB? Senate Bill 210 (Leyva; Chapter 298, Statutes of 2019) directed CARB to develop and implement this regulation. The Board approved the regulation on December 9, 2021.

  3. What are the key components or strategies that the Clean Truck Check program employs to achieve its goals? The program combines periodic vehicle testing requirements with other emissions monitoring techniques. It also includes expanded enforcement strategies to identify vehicles needing emissions-related repairs and ensures those repairs are performed.

  4. When did the implementation of the Clean Truck Check program begin, and what is its expected outcome when fully implemented? Implementation of the Clean Truck Check program began phasing in starting January 2023. When fully implemented, it is expected to provide significant reductions in smog-forming and carcinogenic toxic air pollution, helping achieve federal air quality mandates and healthy air in California.

  5. What is the role of NORCAL CARB MOBILE in the context of the Clean Truck Check program? NORCAL CARB MOBILE provides a mobile Clean Truck Check inspection service for Northern California, offering a convenient way for heavy-duty vehicle owners to meet CARB and Clean Truck Check requirements without needing to visit a physical shop.

  6. Describe the unique selling proposition of NORCAL CARB MOBILE's service for heavy-duty vehicle owners. Their unique selling proposition is bringing certified technicians directly to the customer's location, whether home or work, to perform inspections. This offers convenience, transparent pricing, and reliable service, aimed at building lasting trust.

  7. How does NORCAL CARB MOBILE incentivize referrals for the Clean Truck Check service? NORCAL CARB MOBILE offers a $10 Digital Visa Gift Card for each qualified Clean Truck Check referral that completes testing with them. There is no limit to how many cards a referrer can earn.

  8. What is the minimum cost for large fleets using NORCAL CARB MOBILE's services? NORCAL CARB MOBILE advertises transparent pricing starting as low as $50 for large fleets.

  9. Besides periodic vehicle testing, what other emissions monitoring techniques does the Clean Truck Check program incorporate? In addition to periodic vehicle testing, the Clean Truck Check program incorporates "other emissions monitoring techniques." While specific techniques aren't fully detailed in the provided text, On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) is mentioned as a related program.

  10. Who is the primary contact for inquiries regarding the Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) program at the California Air Resources Board? The primary contact for the Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) program at CARB is hdim@arb.ca.gov via email or phone at (866) 634-3735.

Essay Format Questions

  1. Discuss the legislative and regulatory framework that led to the establishment of California's Clean Truck Check program. What specific environmental goals is this program designed to address, and how does it contribute to broader state and federal air quality mandates?

  2. Analyze the multi-faceted approach of the Clean Truck Check program. Beyond periodic testing, explain the significance of "other emissions monitoring techniques" and "expanded enforcement strategies" in ensuring compliance and achieving emission reductions.

  3. Compare and contrast the roles of a state agency like the California Air Resources Board (CARB) and private sector entities like NORCAL CARB MOBILE in the successful implementation and accessibility of environmental regulations like the Clean Truck Check.

  4. Evaluate the benefits and challenges of a mobile inspection service model, as offered by NORCAL CARB MOBILE, for heavy-duty vehicle owners in California. How does this model address potential barriers to compliance, and what are its limitations?

  5. Consider the economic and environmental impacts of the Clean Truck Check program. Discuss how the program aims to balance the costs of compliance for vehicle owners with the benefits of improved air quality for California communities.

Glossary of Key Terms

  • California Air Resources Board (CARB): A department within the California Environmental Protection Agency responsible for maintaining and improving air quality, and controlling pollution from various sources, including vehicles.

  • Clean Truck Check (HD I/M): The official name for California's Heavy-Duty Vehicle Inspection and Maintenance program, designed to ensure proper functioning of emissions control systems in heavy-duty vehicles.

  • Heavy-Duty Vehicle Inspection and Maintenance (HD I/M): The comprehensive regulatory program developed by CARB to reduce emissions from heavy-duty vehicles through testing, monitoring, and enforcement.

  • Senate Bill 210 (Leyva; Chapter 298, Statutes of 2019): The specific California legislation that directed CARB to develop and implement the Clean Truck Check (HD I/M) regulation.

  • Emissions Control Systems: Components and technologies in a vehicle designed to reduce the output of harmful pollutants from the exhaust.

  • Smog-forming Pollution: Air pollutants that react in the atmosphere to create ground-level ozone, a primary component of smog, which can harm human health and vegetation.

  • Carcinogenic Toxic Air Pollution: Air pollutants that are known or suspected to cause cancer in humans.

  • Federal Air Quality Mandates: Standards and requirements set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for ambient air quality, which states must meet to protect public health and welfare.

  • Periodic Vehicle Testing: Regular, scheduled inspections of vehicles to ensure their emissions control systems are functioning correctly and they meet emissions standards.

  • Emissions Monitoring Techniques: Methods used to track and assess the level of pollutants emitted by vehicles, which can include on-board diagnostics (OBD) and other remote sensing technologies.

  • On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) Program: A system in vehicles that monitors the performance of the engine's components, including those responsible for controlling emissions, and alerts the driver to malfunctions.

  • Credentialed Testers: Individuals who have received specific training and certification to perform Clean Truck Check inspections.

  • Referee Program: A component of the Clean Truck Check program (and similar I/M programs) that provides expert assistance and dispute resolution for complex emissions-related issues or failed tests.

  • NORCAL CARB MOBILE: A private mobile service provider offering Clean Truck Check inspections for heavy-duty vehicles in Northern California.

  • J1667 Smoke Opacity Testing: A specific test method often used for diesel vehicles to measure the opacity (darkness) of exhaust smoke, indicating particulate matter emissions.

title: CARB J1667 Smoke Testing Explained: California's Heavy-Duty Standard
content:
J1667 Smoke Testing Explained: California's Heavy-Duty Standard for older diesel engines.

What Every Heavy-Duty Fleet Owner Needs to Know About J1667 Testing

If you operate heavy-duty diesel vehicles in California, you've likely heard about J1667 smoke testing. But understanding exactly what it entails—and why it's critical for your fleet's compliance—can mean the difference between smooth operations and costly violations.

As California's emissions standards become increasingly stringent, J1667 smoke testing has emerged as the gold standard for heavy-duty vehicle compliance. Here's everything you need to know.

Understanding J1667: The Technical Foundation

J1667 refers to the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) standard for snap-acceleration smoke testing of heavy-duty diesel vehicles. This isn't just bureaucratic paperwork—it's a precise scientific measurement that determines whether your vehicles meet California's strict opacity standards.

What Makes J1667 Different

Unlike basic visual inspections, J1667 testing uses calibrated opacity meters to measure the exact percentage of light blocked by exhaust smoke during acceleration. California requires that diesel vehicles produce no more than:

  • 20% opacity for most heavy-duty vehicles

  • 15% opacity for vehicles with Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR) over 14,000 lbs manufactured after 1991

  • 40% opacity during the acceleration peak (lasting no more than 3 seconds)

The J1667 Testing Process: What to Expect

Pre-Test Vehicle Preparation

Before testing begins, our certified technicians ensure your vehicle meets specific conditions:

  • Engine at normal operating temperature

  • Vehicle positioned on level ground

  • All emission control systems functioning

  • Engine idle speed verified

The Snap-Acceleration Test

The actual J1667 procedure involves:

  1. Baseline Reading: Recording ambient light conditions

  2. Acceleration Sequence: Rapidly accelerating the engine from idle to governed speed

  3. Opacity Measurement: Recording maximum opacity during the acceleration event

  4. Multiple Runs: Conducting three separate tests for accuracy

  5. Pass/Fail Determination: Comparing results against California standards

Total testing time: Approximately 15-20 minutes per vehicle

Why California Mandates J1667 Testing

Environmental Protection

Heavy-duty diesel vehicles are significant contributors to particulate matter (PM) emissions. The J1667 standard helps ensure that:

  • Diesel particulate filters (DPF) are functioning properly

  • Engine combustion remains efficient

  • Emission control systems haven't been tampered with

Public Health Compliance

California's stringent standards directly impact air quality in major metropolitan areas. J1667 testing helps protect public health by:

  • Reducing respiratory irritants

  • Minimizing particulate pollution

  • Supporting the state's climate goals

J1667 smoke testing is required for:

  • Fleet vehicles subject to CARB regulations

title: Clean Truck Check Requirements for Personal (Non-Commercial) Use Motorhomes
content:
Clean Truck Check Requirements for Personal (Non-Commercial) Use Motorhomes

Clean Truck Check is a CARB program to ensure emissions control equipment is operating properly for heavy-duty vehicles if your motor home is:  

  • Over 14,000 lbs. manufacturer’s Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR)

    • The manufacturer's original GVWR label is commonly found on the vehicle’s door jamb or on the inside of the door

  • Non-gasoline

  • Registered in California

Reporting

The Clean Truck Check Vehicle Inspection System (CTC-VIS) is the reporting system where you enter information about the owner and the vehicle. It is also the place to make the compliance fee payments.

  • Complete instructions are available at Clean Truck Check - Reporting Resources (CTC-VIS).

Annual Fee Payment

Annual compliance fees need to be paid by the vehicle’s compliance deadline, which is shown in CTC-VIS, and must be paid in CTC-VIS.  

  • Payments must be made through your CTC-VIS account and may only be made by debit card, credit card, or eCheck. 

    • Paper checks will not be accepted.   

Emissions Compliance Testing

Testing deadlines begin January 1, 2025, and require submission of a passing Clean Truck Check emissions compliance test by a credentialed tester. Testing may be done up to 90 days prior to the vehicle’s testing compliance deadline to allow time for any necessary repairs.

  • Your testing deadlines will be visible in your CTC VIS account 

  • Compliance deadlines are the same as your DMV registration due date.

  • You may search for credentialed testers by location at Available for Hire Credentialed Testers.

How must my motor home be tested? 

More information is available at Clean Truck Check - Emissions Compliance Testing Requirements.

OBD-equipped vehicles (2013 and newer diesel engines and 2018 and newer alternative fuel engines) are required to undergo a scan of the engine’s OBD data using a California Air Resources Board (CARB) certified OBD testing device. 

Diesel non-OBD vehicles (2012 and older diesel engines) are required to complete a smoke opacity test and a visual inspection of the vehicle’s emissions control equipment, referred to in this program as the Vehicle Emissions Control Equipment Inspection. Alternative fuel non-OBD vehicles (2017 and older alternative fuel engines) are only required to complete the visual inspection and are not subject to the smoke opacity test requirement.

California DMV Registration Holds Labeled Clean Truck Check (SB 210) 

The California Department of Motor Vehicles (CA DMV) is currently imposing registration holds on vehicles that do not comply with the Clean Truck Check program. To prevent potential registration holds, ensure that your vehicle is registered in CTC-VIS, all annual compliance fees are paid, and that all annual emissions compliance testing requirements have been fulfilled.

For non-commercial motorhomes, including those used for recreational or emergency purposes, please adhere to the compliance requirements outlined in the "California Motorhomes Annual Compliance Requirements."

Commercial motorhomes should consult the "Clean Truck Check Semi-Annual Compliance Requirements" for applicable compliance standards.

Contact

If you have questions, contact support@norcalcabmobile.com or 916-890-4427

title: CARB Compliance in Napa Valley: Keeping Your Fleet Road-Ready in Wine Country
content:
The Napa Valley region is home to world-renowned wineries, thriving nonprofits, agricultural operations, and essential service providers—all of which depend on commercial vehicles to keep their operations running smoothly. Whether you're delivering wine to distributors, transporting produce to market, or serving the community like the Napa Food Bank, maintaining CARB compliance isn't just a legal requirement—it's essential to keeping your business moving forward.

title: The 2025 CARB Clean Truck Check: Complete Compliance Guide
content:
Everything Northern California fleet operators need to know about CARB Clean Truck Check requirements, testing options, costs, and how to stay compliant without losing a single hour of productivity.

Quick Answer: The CARB Clean Truck Check requires California diesel trucks (14,001+ lbs) to pass emissions tests 2-4 times per year starting in 2025. Tests cost $75-$250 depending on engine age. Non-compliance results in $800+ daily fines and DMV registration holds. Mobile testing services eliminate the 4+ hours of downtime required for fixed station visits.

Fleets must submit passing tests 2× in 2025, increasing to 4× in 2026, within designated compliance windows.

The Hidden Cost: 4+ Hours Lost Per Test

Beyond fines, the biggest expense is operational downtime. Taking a truck to a fixed testing station means pulling a driver off a route, fighting traffic, and waiting in line.

A 10-truck fleet needing 4 tests/year = 160 hours of lost productivity annually

The 3-Step Compliance Process

Staying compliant involves more than just a test. Follow this process for every truck in your fleet.

1

Register in CTC-VIS

All vehicles must be registered in the CARB Clean Truck Check Vehicle Information System (CTC-VIS) database. Pay annual compliance fees and ensure each vehicle's VIN is properly recorded.

2

Identify Your Test Type

2013+ model year engines: OBD (On-Board Diagnostics) test - connects to your truck's computer to check emissions codes.

2012 and older engines: J1667 Smoke Opacity Test - measures exhaust smoke density.

3

Submit Passing Tests

Submit passing tests 2× per year in 2025 (increasing to 4× in 2026) within your assigned compliance windows. A failed test or missed deadline immediately triggers non-compliance status.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the CARB Clean Truck Check?

The CARB Clean Truck Check is California's heavy-duty vehicle inspection program that requires diesel trucks (14,001+ lbs GVWR) to pass emissions tests 2-4 times per year starting in 2025. Tests include OBD diagnostics for 2013+ engines or smoke opacity testing for older engines. Non-compliance results in DMV registration holds and fines exceeding $800 per day.

How much does CARB testing cost?

Testing costs vary by type: OBD testing for 2013+ engines costs $75, J1667 smoke opacity testing for 2012 and older engines costs $250, and RV/motorhome testing costs $300. Mobile on-site testing eliminates hidden costs like driver wages ($100+) and fuel for trips to fixed stations.

How often do trucks need testing?

Starting in 2025, trucks require testing 2 times per year. In 2026, this increases to 4 times per year. Each vehicle must submit passing tests within designated compliance windows to avoid DMV holds and fines.

What happens if my truck fails?

If your truck fails, you must repair the emissions system and pass a retest within 30 days. Continued non-compliance results in DMV registration holds, fines exceeding $800 per violation per day, and potential roadside citations. Your vehicle may be prohibited from operating in California.

OBD vs. smoke opacity: What's the difference?

OBD testing connects to your truck's computer to read emissions codes (required for 2013+ engines). Smoke opacity testing measures exhaust smoke density with a specialized meter (required for 2012 and older engines). Both are submitted to CARB's CTC-VIS database for compliance.

Can testing be done at my location?

Yes. Mobile CARB testing services come directly to your yard, warehouse, or job site. This eliminates 4+ hours of downtime per truck by testing during off-hours while trucks aren't running—saving driver wages, fuel costs, and lost revenue.

Which vehicles are covered?

CARB Clean Truck Check applies to diesel-powered vehicles with GVWR of 14,001+ pounds operating in California: semi-trucks, box trucks, dump trucks, buses, RVs/motorhomes, and agricultural equipment. Out-of-state trucks operating in California must also comply.

title: What Is the Clean Truck Check? California’s New HD I/M Program Explained
content:
California’s transportation regulations just got stricter — and if you operate diesel trucks over 14,000 pounds, you’re directly affected. The Clean Truck Check, also known as the Heavy-Duty Inspection and Maintenance (HD I/M) Program, is the California Air Resources Board’s (CARB) newest compliance program aimed at reducing pollution from heavy-duty vehicles.


In this post, we’ll break down everything fleet managers, owner-operators, and businesses need to know — and how NorCal CARB Mobile can help you stay compliant with convenient, mobile HD I/M testing services.

What Is the Clean Truck Check?



The Clean Truck Check is CARB’s statewide emissions inspection program for non-gasoline heavy-duty vehicles with a GVWR over 14,000 pounds. Think of it as California’s version of a smog check — but for big rigs, box trucks, buses, and other diesel-powered commercial vehicles.


It ensures that heavy-duty trucks operating in California are properly maintained and not releasing excessive emissions, particularly particulate matter (PM) and NOx (nitrogen oxides) — two major contributors to air pollution.


Who Must Comply?

The program applies to:


  • Diesel trucks over 14,000 lbs GVWR

  • Alternative fuel and hybrid trucks (non-gasoline)

  • California-registered and out-of-state trucks operating in California

  • Single owner-operators and fleets alike



Whether your vehicle is based in Sacramento, San Jose, or out of state, if it’s working on California roads, it’s subject to HD I/M testing requirements.


What Does the Program Require?



There are three major components of Clean Truck Check compliance:


  1. Vehicle Emissions Control Equipment Inspection

    Testers verify that emission control systems (like diesel particulate filters and SCR systems) are properly installed and functioning.

  2. SAE J1667 Smoke Opacity Test

    This test measures the amount of visible smoke a vehicle emits — an indicator of poor combustion or equipment failure.

  3. OBD Testing (for newer vehicles)

    Vehicles with onboard diagnostic systems must pass periodic digital scans.


When and How Often Do You Need to Test?



Testing frequency depends on vehicle model year and registration status. Most vehicles will need semi-annual testing, with additional roadside or surprise inspections possible.


Avoiding or delaying testing can lead to fines, registration blocks, or DMV holds.


Why Mobile HD I/M Testing Makes Sense



At NorCal CARB Mobile, we take the hassle out of compliance. No waiting in line at a test facility or taking vehicles out of service. Our mobile CARB-certified testers come to your location—whether it’s a fleet yard, construction site, or your driveway.


We serve:

What Makes NorCal CARB Mobile Different?



✅ On-site smoke opacity and emissions control inspections

✅ CARB-certified technicians

✅ Fast scheduling and digital compliance reporting

✅ Transparent pricing with no surprise fees

✅ Fleet testing and single vehicle testing available




Ready to Schedule Your Clean Truck Check?

Unfortunately, many fleets still treat CARB compliance as an afterthought. Inspections are rushed at the last minute, paperwork is scrambled together, and every surprise compliance issue means more downtime and unplanned expenses.

The Complexity of CARB Compliance

CARB’s Heavy-Duty Inspection and Maintenance (HD I/M) program demands regular emissions testing, data submissions, and record keeping. Fleets must track each vehicle’s compliance schedule, ensure all diagnostic equipment is calibrated, and handle issues like failed tests or missing data immediately. When these tasks rely on manual processes, it’s easy for things to fall through the cracks. Deadlines slip, paperwork gets lost, and issues are noticed only when a vehicle is already due for a test.

The Role of Automation

Automation changes the game. By integrating sensors, telematics, and cloud-based platforms, fleets can continuously monitor emissions performance, receive alerts when maintenance is needed, and schedule testing automatically. Automated systems can pull vehicle data, match it to CARB reporting requirements, and populate the necessary forms. This reduces the administrative burden on operators and ensures timely submissions. When problems arise – whether it’s a high NOx reading or a missed test window – automation triggers workflows for resolution rather than letting the issue sit unnoticed.

title: Your VIN Just Triggered a Compliance Deadline: Find Your Clean Truck Check Due Date
content:
The aggressive enforcement of the CARB Clean Truck Check program means deadlines are no longer optional—they are critical to staying on the road. If you're a California-registered fleet or an out-of-state hauler, your vehicle requires semi-annual testing in 2025 and 2026.

The critical question is: When is your test due?

How CARB Determines Your Due Date

Your truck's specific compliance deadline is determined by its registration status:

  1. California-Registered Vehicles: Your compliance dates are based on your DMV registration expiration date. Generally, you have two deadlines: the month of your registration expiration and six months after that date.

  2. Non-California Registered Vehicles: Your compliance dates are determined by the last digit of your Vehicle Identification Number (VIN). For example, a VIN ending in '4' has due dates in February and August.

  3. Motorhomes & RVs: If registered in California and over 14,000 lbs, you must still register and pay the fee, but you only require testing once per year (annually).

Why You Can't Wait for the Due Date

You can (and should) submit your passing emissions test up to 90 days before your compliance deadline. Testing early minimizes risk. If your vehicle fails, you need time for repairs and a retest, which must all be completed before the deadline to avoid fines and DMV holds.

The clearest way to find your exact due date is to check your official CTC-VIS account. If you can't access it, call us—we help fleets navigate compliance schedules every day.

title: Accuracy Matters in CARB Testing, Especially When the Weather Doesn’t Cooperate
content:
If you’ve lived in California long enough, you know how unpredictable our weather can be. One week we’re praying for rain. The next, we’re watching an atmospheric river roll in from San Francisco, stretch through the Valley, and reach as far north as Chico. This week is one of those moments, with steady rain expected for days on end.




At NorCal CARB Mobile, weather like this doesn’t change our priorities. It just means we have to be even more diligent.


We’re a fully mobile emissions testing company, serving customers from San Jose to Sacramento, and our job is simple in theory but demanding in practice: deliver accurate, compliant smoke opacity and OBD tests, no matter where we’re working.

Why Environment Matters in Smoke Opacity Testing

Smoke opacity testing is not just about plugging in a machine and hitting “go.” Instruments like the Wager 6500 and 6500+ are highly precise optical devices. They measure how much light is blocked by diesel exhaust particles during a snap-acceleration test.


That precision means environmental conditions matter.


Heavy rain, fog, mist, wind, and even high humidity can interfere with the optical path between the sensor and the exhaust plume. Rain droplets and airborne moisture can scatter light and introduce noise into the reading. That doesn’t mean the engine suddenly got dirtier. It means the environment can distort the measurement if the test isn’t handled correctly.


California regulations and SAE J1667 standards don’t prohibit testing in rain, but they do require valid, repeatable results. If the readings don’t meet strict consistency criteria, the test isn’t valid. Running more tests doesn’t fix that. Good judgment does.



How We Handle Challenging Conditions



Being mobile means we see it all. Clear Central Valley mornings, coastal humidity in the Bay Area, and now multi-day rain events driven by atmospheric rivers.


Our approach is straightforward:


  • We verify calibration before testing, especially in wet or unstable weather.

  • We make sure exhaust rain caps are fully open and that water isn’t crossing the optical path.

  • We pay close attention to wind direction, shelter, and positioning to avoid plume disruption.

  • If conditions are unstable and valid readings can’t be achieved, we pause or reschedule rather than force a questionable result.



We always err on the side of safety, accuracy, and compliance. That protects our customers and keeps their vehicles on the right side of CARB requirements.



Mobile Doesn’t Mean Cutting Corners



At NorCalCARBMobile.com, being mobile means bringing the same level of care and technical discipline to your location that you’d expect from a fixed testing site.


Whether we’re performing a smoke opacity test with a Wager 6500 or running standard OBD diagnostics, our goal is the same every time: accurate data, proper procedure, and clear communication.


California’s weather may swing from drought to downpour, but our standards don’t change. Rain or shine, from San Jose to Sacramento, we’re here to help our customers stay compliant and confident in their results.


If you ever have questions about testing conditions, timing, or what to expect during a weather event like this one, just ask. We believe informed customers are better protected customers.


And when the rain finally stops, we’ll still be rolling.

title: Is CARB (Clean Truck Check) testing four times this year?
content:
Beginning October 2027, on-board diagnostics (OBD) equipped vehicles will be required to undergo testing four times per year.  This does not apply to Non-Commercial Motorhomes or Agricultural vehicles as defined in the Heavy Duty Inspection and Maintenance regulation; they will remain on an annual testing schedule.

What are the deadlines for my vehicle’s emissions compliance testing? 

The compliance deadlines will be available in the owner’s Clean Truck Check-Vehicle Inspection System (CTC-VIS) account for each vehicle.
View this video for instructions of where to find your test due dates and results. View Test Details.

Information on annual and semi-annual testing requirements, including how to determine each vehicle’s deadline(s), is available below:

  • Clean Truck Check Requirements for Vehicles Subject to Semi-Annual Compliance 

  • Agricultural Vehicles & California Motorhomes Annual Compliance Requirements 

Passing NorCal CARB Mobile Clean Truck Check test results may be submitted up to 90 days before a compliance deadline. Testing deadlines for each vehicle will be shown in your CTC-VIS account and a monthly account summary will be emailed at the beginning of each month listing the number of vehicles with a compliance deadline in the next 90 days and the number of any open enforcement actions.

title: 2026 CARB Testing Deadlines for Fleet Operators
content:
If you operate a fleet of diesel trucks in California, 2026 brings tighter compliance requirements under the CARB Clean Truck Check program. The testing frequency has increased, penalties are steeper, and CARB is actively auditing fleet compliance through the CTC-VIS system.

Here is what fleet operators need to know to stay compliant and avoid costly penalties this year.

Twice-yearly testing is now standard

Starting in 2026, CARB requires two Clean Truck Check tests per year for most commercial diesel vehicles. This is an increase from the previous annual testing requirement and applies to:

  • All Class 4 through Class 8 diesel trucks
  • Diesel buses and transit vehicles
  • Vehicles with prior compliance failures
  • Fleets flagged for audit by CARB

The two testing windows are typically spaced six months apart. Your specific deadlines are assigned through CTC-VIS based on your vehicle registration date. If you are unsure of your testing window, check your CTC-VIS portal or contact us and we can help you look it up.

Key dates for 2026

While individual vehicle deadlines vary, here are the general compliance milestones fleet operators should be aware of:

  • January 1, 2026: Twice-yearly testing requirement takes effect for most fleets
  • Q1 and Q2 testing windows: First-half tests must be completed by the deadline assigned in CTC-VIS (typically within your registration month)
  • Q3 and Q4 testing windows: Second-half tests due six months after your first-half deadline
  • December 31, 2026: All vehicles must have completed both required tests for the year

Missing either testing window puts you out of compliance and exposes you to penalties.

Penalties for non-compliance

CARB has increased enforcement in 2026. The penalties for failing to complete required Clean Truck Check testing include:

  • $1,000 per vehicle for first-time non-compliance
  • $2,500+ per vehicle for repeat violations
  • Registration holds — DMV will not renew registration for non-compliant vehicles
  • Roadside enforcement — CHP can cite non-compliant vehicles during inspections

For a fleet of 10 trucks, a single missed testing cycle could result in $10,000 or more in fines — far more than the cost of testing.

How to stay ahead of deadlines

The simplest way to avoid compliance problems is to schedule testing proactively rather than waiting for your deadline to approach. Here are practical steps:

1. Know your CTC-VIS deadlines

Log in to your CTC-VIS portal and note the testing deadline for every vehicle in your fleet. If you have vehicles with different deadline months, create a calendar with all dates.

2. Schedule testing 30 days early

Do not wait until the deadline month. Schedule your testing at least 30 days before the due date. This gives you time for retests if any vehicle fails, and avoids the rush of other operators trying to test at the last minute.

3. Use mobile testing to minimize downtime

For fleets, mobile testing eliminates the biggest compliance bottleneck: getting trucks off their routes to visit a testing center. With mobile testing, a certified tester comes to your yard and tests every vehicle on-site — typically under two hours for a fleet of six trucks.

4. Consider a managed compliance program

NorCal CARB Mobile offers a Full Care compliance program for $40 per vehicle per year. We track your CTC-VIS deadlines, send reminders before each testing window, and schedule your mobile testing appointments automatically. You never miss a deadline because we are watching them for you.

What if you are already behind?

title: How Mobile CARB Testing Works
content:
If you have ever wondered what actually happens when a mobile CARB tester shows up to your yard, this walkthrough covers the entire process from first call to compliance confirmation.

The short version: you call or submit a request, we show up at your location, test your trucks on-site, and handle the CARB paperwork. Most fleet operators are done in under two hours with zero downtime beyond the test itself.

Step 1: Book your appointment

Getting started takes about two minutes. You can submit a request online or call (916) 890-4427 directly. We need three things:

  • Your location — the address where the trucks are parked
  • Number of vehicles — how many trucks, buses, or motorhomes need testing
  • Engine years — this determines whether each vehicle needs OBD or OVI testing

We schedule appointments Monday through Saturday across our service area: Sacramento, Stockton, Fairfield, San Jose, and the entire Bay Area. Most appointments are available within the same week.

Step 2: We come to your yard

On the scheduled day, our certified tester arrives at your location with all necessary equipment. There is nothing you need to prepare beyond having the vehicles accessible — engines off, keys available.

For OBD testing (2013+ engines), the tester connects a diagnostic tool to each vehicle's OBD port, typically located under the dashboard. The tool reads emissions-related fault codes from the engine control module. The entire process takes about 10 to 15 minutes per vehicle.

For OVI testing (pre-2013 engines), the tester uses a calibrated opacity meter positioned at the exhaust outlet. The engine is run through a series of controlled snap-accelerations while the meter measures smoke density. This takes about 15 to 20 minutes per vehicle.

Step 3: Results on the spot

You get your pass or fail result immediately after each vehicle is tested. There is no waiting period — the diagnostic readout or opacity measurement gives a definitive answer on the spot.

If a vehicle passes, you are done with that truck. The compliance record is submitted to CARB electronically.

If a vehicle fails, the tester can typically identify the root cause during the test. Common issues include DPF (diesel particulate filter) problems, faulty O2 sensors, exhaust leaks, or engine control module faults. You get a clear explanation of what needs repair and can schedule a retest after the work is done.

Step 4: CARB paperwork handled

After testing, we submit your results to CARB through the CTC-VIS (Clean Truck Check Vehicle Information System) portal. You do not need to file anything separately or visit a CARB office. Your compliance status updates automatically in the system.

You receive a test report for your records, and your vehicles are marked compliant in the CARB database. If you are enrolled in our Full Care program, we also track your next testing deadline and send reminders before it comes due.

What about fleet testing?

Fleet testing follows the same process — we just test multiple vehicles in sequence at the same location. This is where mobile testing provides the biggest advantage over stationary centers.

At a testing center, each truck has to be driven there individually, wait in line, get tested, and driven back. For a fleet of six trucks, that can consume an entire workday across multiple drivers.

With mobile testing, we come to your yard once and test all six trucks in sequence. Total time on-site: typically under two hours. Your drivers stay on their regular routes or assignments — no one has to make a trip to a testing center.

What does it cost?

NorCal CARB Mobile charges per vehicle with no trip fees or hidden costs:

  • OBD test: $75 per vehicle
  • OVI (smoke) test: $199 per vehicle
  • Motorhome OBD: $99 per vehicle
  • Motorhome OVI: $229 per vehicle

Fleet and multi-truck discounts are available. Contact us for a quote.

Ready to schedule?

Most appointments are available within the same week, including Saturdays. Book a test or call (916) 890-4427.

title: What Is the CARB Clean Truck Check?
content:
If you own or operate a diesel truck, bus, or motorhome in California, the CARB Clean Truck Check program applies to you. Launched by the California Air Resources Board (CARB), the program requires periodic emissions testing to ensure heavy-duty diesel vehicles meet the state's air quality standards.

Whether you run a single box truck or a fleet of semis, understanding what testing you need — and when — is essential to avoiding fines and keeping your vehicles on the road.

Two types of testing: OBD and OVI

The Clean Truck Check program uses two distinct testing methods, depending on your vehicle's engine year and configuration.

OBD (On-Board Diagnostics) testing

OBD testing applies to most diesel vehicles with engines from 2013 and newer. The test connects to your truck's OBD port — the same diagnostic port your mechanic uses — and reads emissions-related fault codes directly from the engine control module.

The test takes about 10 to 15 minutes per vehicle. If your OBD system reports no active emissions faults, you pass. It is the faster and less expensive of the two methods — $75 per vehicle with NorCal CARB Mobile.

OVI (Opacity / Visual Inspection) testing

OVI testing, also called a smoke opacity test or SAE J1667 test, applies to vehicles with pre-2013 engines or engines that do not have a compatible OBD system. This test measures the density of smoke coming from the exhaust under a controlled snap-acceleration procedure.

A certified tester uses a calibrated opacity meter to measure the smoke level as a percentage. If the reading is below the allowable threshold, you pass. OVI testing costs $199 per vehicle with NorCal CARB Mobile.

Who needs a Clean Truck Check?

The program applies broadly to heavy-duty diesel vehicles operating in California:

  • Class 4 through Class 8 diesel trucks (over 14,000 lbs GVWR)
  • Diesel buses and transit vehicles
  • Diesel motorhomes and RVs
  • Agricultural diesel vehicles (with some exemptions)
  • Out-of-state trucks operating in California

If your vehicle has a diesel engine and operates on California roads, you almost certainly need periodic Clean Truck Check testing. The consequences of non-compliance include fines starting at $1,000 per vehicle and potential registration holds.

How often do you need testing?

As of 2026, CARB requires testing at least once per year for most vehicles, with some fleet operators required to test twice per year. Testing frequency depends on your vehicle's compliance history and fleet size.

CARB tracks compliance through the CTC-VIS (Clean Truck Check Vehicle Information System) portal. Your test results are submitted electronically by your tester — you do not need to file anything separately.

Mobile testing vs. stationary testing centers

You have two options for getting tested: drive to a stationary testing center, or have a mobile tester come to you.

For fleet operators with multiple trucks, mobile testing saves hours of downtime. Instead of driving each truck to a center and waiting in line, a certified tester comes to your yard and tests every vehicle on-site. A fleet of six trucks that would take an entire day at a testing center can be done in under two hours at your location.

NorCal CARB Mobile provides on-site testing across Sacramento, Stockton, Fairfield, San Jose, and the Bay Area — including Saturday appointments.

What happens if you fail?

If your vehicle fails its Clean Truck Check, you are given a window to make repairs and retest. Common reasons for failure include faulty sensors, exhaust leaks, DPF (diesel particulate filter) issues, and engine control module faults.

Your tester can often identify the specific issue during testing and point you toward the right repair. After repairs, you schedule a retest to clear the failure and restore compliance.

Get tested

NorCal CARB Mobile makes compliance straightforward. We come to your yard, test your vehicles on-site, and handle the CARB paperwork. OBD testing starts at $75, OVI at $199, and we offer fleet discounts for multi-truck yards.
